justification: The Coral/Algae class in the deep lagoon. The Coral/Algae class has a hard underlying framework (usually limestone) with a covering of coral (including soft coral) and/or seaweeds (including macroalgae and turf algae). Coral/Algae is topographically complex and supports high animal diversity and biomass. Both corals and algae are photosynthetic and have similar spectral signatures, making them difficult to distinguish by remote sensing.
A Seagrass dominated habitat on a soft bottom in the deep lagoon. From the Allen Coral Atlas.
Rock is a hard substrate that provides habitat for sessile marine life. From the Allen Coral Watch Atlas.

Rubble is a natural part of a coral reef ecosystem, comprising broken dead coral skeletons and rock fragments. It is created by physical disturbances such as storms and anchor damage, and provides some habitat for sessile marine life. From the Allen Coral Watch Atlas.
Sand is a soft-bottom area with fine, unconsolidated sediments. It has animals living on the surface (epifauna) and in the sediment (infauna). From the Allen Coral Watch Atlas.
SSP2-4.5 is an intermediate greenhouse gas emission scenario, with CO2 emissions around current levels until 2050, then falling but not reaching net zero by 2100.
SSP3-7.0 is a high greenhouse gas emission scenario, with CO2 emissions doubling by 2100.
SSP5-8.5 is a very high greenhouse gas emission scenario, with CO2 emissions tripling by 2075.
SSP5-8.5 is a very high greenhouse gas emission scenario, with CO2 emissions tripling by 2075.

Uses the distance from the coast as the cost layer, with distances further from the coast having higher cost. This mirrors the extra fuel consumption, longer travel time, and greater wear on the engine.

No cost layer is used. In this instance, the size of the planning units is used as the cost, but because (usually) all planning units have the same size, the cost of selecting any planning unit is the same.
The number of fishing hours, measured in hours of inferred fishing activity per square kilometer, from AIS data on fishing vessels. From Global Fishing Watch.

The upper 0-200 m in the ocean. It contains all primary producers in the ocean, and most of the marine life is restricted to this depth or vertically migrates into this depth zone (e.g. seabirds, sea turtles, tuna).
Between 200-1000 m deep. Also known as the twilight zone because of the dim light conditions. Contains unique biodiversity.
Aquaculture is the farming of marine and freshwater finfish, shellfish, aquatic plants, algae and other organisms. It is an important source of revenue in many nations in the Global South.
